text:
High-valent iron-oxo species are proposed to be key intermediates in enzymes that activate C–H bonds, but mimicking their high-spin ($S = 2$) state and reactivity remains an enormous synthetic challenge, due in part to the instability of the iron-oxo terminal motif to oligomerization and ligand oxidation reactions. We report here that the metal-organic framework material $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ and its magnesium-diluted form $\text{Fe}_{0.1}\text{Mg}_{1.9}\text{(dobdc)}$ offer a route to encapsulating and activating the iron sites.

We demonstrate that with nitrous oxide as terminal oxidant, ethane is converted to ethanol and acetaldehyde at relatively low temperatures. Detailed structural information is provided on the frameworks before and after activation through powder neutron diffraction and infrared spectroscopy. We reveal that N2O is weakly coordinated to the iron(II) centres within a bent structure. Upon activation, a short-lived iron(IV)-oxo intermediate is proposed while the material recovers to form a stable iron(III)-hydroxide species anchored to the $\text{Fe}_2\text{(dobdc)}$ motif. The electronic structure of the reactive iron(IV)-oxo species has been studied using hybrid density functional theory (DFT) as well as multireference perturbation theory at the CASPT2 level. Our studies reveal that the high-spin iron(IV) centre of the reactive intermediate adopts a $S = 2$ ground state. The MOF therefore demonstrates the possibility of stabilising unusual electronic configurations and promotes the functionalisation of strong C–H bonds.
explanation: Contains highly specific, field-accurate details (Fe2(dobdc), N2O activation, CASPT2, S = 2) that are typical of human-written research abstracts. While AI can generate such content, the exacting experimental and computational references make it plausible as human-authored, yielding a moderate AI-detection probability.
